title: Long QT Syndrome Type 2
chief_complaint: I passed out while swimming yesterday. I was doing laps and suddenly everything went black. The lifeguard pulled me out. I woke up on the pool deck confused. This has happened before - once when I was startled by an alarm clock, and once during a swimming race in high school.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Bradycardia 58 bpm. Regular. No murmurs. Soft S2. No clicks or gallops.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Normal. No focal deficits. No tongue laceration (but could be absence seizure vs arrhythmia).

hidden_data.ecg: Sinus bradycardia 58 bpm. QTc 520ms (Bazett formula) - severely prolonged (normal <460ms in females). T wave notching in lateral leads (V4-V6) - classic for LQT2. T wave alternans present.
hidden_data.stress_test: Exercise: HR increases appropriately. QTc paradoxically lengthens during exercise recovery (classic for LQT2). No arrhythmias induced.
hidden_data.echo: Structurally normal heart. EF 62%. No valvular abnormalities.
hidden_data.genetic_test: KCNH2 mutation (HERG potassium channel) - pathogenic variant confirming LQT2
hidden_data.ecg_parents: Mother: QTc 490ms (borderline prolonged). T wave morphology similar to daughter. Likely carrier.

correct_diagnosis: Long QT Syndrome Type 2 (KCNH2 Mutation) with Recurrent Cardiac Syncope (Torsades de Pointes)
key_learning_points: Long QT syndrome = prolonged ventricular repolarization → risk of Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT) → syncope, seizures, or sudden cardiac death
QTc normal: <450ms males, <460ms females. QTc >500ms = high-risk
LQT2 (KCNH2) triggers: Sudden loud noises (alarm clock, phone ringing), emotional stress, swimming. This patient: syncope triggered by startle AND swimming = classic LQT2
T wave morphology can suggest subtype: LQT1 = broad-based T waves, LQT2 = notched/bifid T waves in lateral leads, LQT3 = late-peaked T waves

Treatment: Beta-blockers (Nadolol or Propranolol) are first-line. ICD for high-risk (prior cardiac arrest, syncope on beta-blocker). Left cardiac sympathetic denervation (LCSD) for refractory cases. Avoid QT-prolonging drugs (www.crediblemeds.org)

patient_responses.syncope: Each time it happened with a sudden loud noise or during swimming. I don't convulse or bite my tongue. I wake up within 30 seconds feeling tired but normal. No confusion afterward.
patient_responses.swimming: I'm a competitive swimmer. The first episode was during a race at age 16. Yesterday was my third episode. Each time was during or immediately after swimming.
patient_responses.family: My mother has 'seizures' that started in her 30s. She takes medication for them. No one else in the family has heart problems that I know of.

feedback.correct_message: Excellent! You identified LQT2 from the classic triggers (swimming + auditory startle), severely prolonged QTc (520ms), notched T waves in lateral leads, and confirmed KCNH2 mutation. The recurrent syncopal episodes are likely Torsades de Pointes. Nadolol is first-line; ICD may be warranted given recurrent symptoms on therapy.
feedback.attending_note: Start Nadolol 40mg daily (or Propranolol LA 80mg), titrate to 2mg/kg/day. Avoid all QT-prolonging drugs (www.crediblemeds.org). No competitive swimming - LQT2 has highest risk during swimming. No swimming alone. ICD implantation if syncope recurs on adequate beta-blocker dose. Screen all first-degree relatives with ECG. Mother likely affected given QTc 490ms. Genetic cascade testing for family. Medical alert bracelet.
